title: ONGC Physics — Full-length Mock 07
pattern: ONGC CBT — 85 MCQ · 120 min · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The vector identity ∇×(∇×A) = ∇(∇·A) − ∇²A is essential in deriving electromagnetic wave equations. This identity is commonly called the:

figure.markup: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 300 100" font-family="serif" font-size="13"><text x="150" y="20" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-style="italic">Curl-Curl Identity</text><text x="20" y="50" font-size="13">∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − ∇²A</text><text x="20" y="80" font-size="11" fill="#555">Separates double curl into gradient-of-divergence and vector Laplacian.</text></svg>

questions.solution: The identity ∇×(∇×A) = ∇(∇·A) − ∇²A is universally called the curl-curl identity. It separates the double curl into a gradient-of-divergence term and a vector Laplacian term, and is used to derive the electromagnetic wave equation from Maxwell's equations.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Joukowski transformation w = z + c²/z (c > 0) maps a circle |z| = R in the z-plane (with R > c) to what curve in the w-plane?

figure.markup: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 400 180" font-family="serif" font-size="11"><text x="200" y="18" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13">Joukowski Transformation: w = z + c²/z</text><ellipse cx="90" cy="100" rx="55" ry="55" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="90" y="170" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">z-plane: circle |z| = R</text><line x1="195" y1="60" x2="310" y2="100" stroke="#999" stroke-width="0.8" stroke-dasharray="4"/><polygon points="305,97 310,100 305,103" fill="#999"/><text x="260" y="50" text-anchor="middle" fill="#999" font-size="10">w = f(z)</text><ellipse cx="310" cy="100" rx="70" ry="35" fill="none" stroke="#c00" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="310" y="170" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">w-plane: ellipse</text></svg>

questions.solution: Substituting z = Re^{iθ}: w = Re^{iθ} + (c²/R)e^{−iθ} = (R + c²/R)cosθ + i(R − c²/R)sinθ. This parametrizes an ellipse with semi-axes a = R + c²/R and b = |R − c²/R|, confirming the Joukowski map sends circles to ellipses.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The algebraic identity R_{ijkl} + R_{iklj} + R_{iljk} = 0 for the Riemann curvature tensor is known as:
questions.solution: The first Bianchi identity states that the Riemann tensor is antisymmetric under cyclic permutation of its last three indices: R_{ijkl} + R_{iklj} + R_{iljk} = 0. This is an algebraic (not differential) identity that follows from the symmetry of the connection.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Laplace transform of the zeroth-order Bessel function J₀(t) is:
questions.solution: Using the integral representation J₀(t) = (1/π)∫₀^π cos(t sin φ)dφ and evaluating the Laplace integral, one obtains ℒ{J₀(t)} = 1/√(s² + 1) for Re(s) > 0. This is a standard transform pair.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The period T of a simple pendulum with large amplitude θ₀ can be expanded as a series in θ₀. The first correction to the small-angle period T₀ = 2π√(l/g) is:
questions.solution: The exact period is T = 4√(l/g)·K(sin(θ₀/2)), where K is the complete elliptic integral of the first kind. Expanding K(k) ≈ (π/2)(1 + k²/4 + 9k⁴/64 + ...) with k ≈ θ₀/2 gives T ≈ T₀(1 + θ₀²/16 + ...). The θ₀²/16 term is the leading anharmonic correction.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A body falling freely from rest in the Northern Hemisphere experiences a horizontal deflection due to the Coriolis force. The deflection is directed:
questions.solution: For a body falling with velocity v = −v_z ẑ, the Coriolis acceleration is a_cor = −2Ω × v. With Ω having a northward horizontal component Ω cos φ and vertical component Ω sin φ, the cross product yields a_cor = 2Ωv_z cos φ x̂ (eastward). Thus the falling body deflects east.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: According to Noether's theorem, conservation of angular momentum in a central force problem is associated with invariance of the Lagrangian under:
questions.solution: Noether's theorem states: every continuous symmetry of the Lagrangian corresponds to a conserved quantity. Rotational invariance (the Lagrangian does not change under rotation of coordinates) yields conservation of angular momentum. Time translation gives energy conservation; spatial translation gives momentum conservation.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Classical Mechanics · Kepler Problem · Tisserand Parameter
questions.stem: The Tisserand parameter T_J is a quasi-conserved quantity in the circular restricted three-body problem. For a body with semi-major axis a, eccentricity e, and inclination i relative to Jupiter's orbit, T_J is approximately:
questions.solution: The Tisserand parameter is T_J = a_J/a + 2√(a/a_J)√(1−e²) cos i. It is approximately conserved in the restricted three-body problem and is used to identify comets related to a particular parent body. For nearly circular orbits (e ≈ 0), it simplifies to a_J/a + 2√(a/a_J) cos i.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The characteristic impedance Z₀ of a lossless transmission line with inductance L per unit length and capacitance C per unit length is:

figure.markup: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 320 100" font-family="serif" font-size="12"><text x="160" y="18" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13">Lossless Transmission Line</text><line x1="30" y1="40" x2="290" y2="40" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="30" y1="75" x2="290" y2="75" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="35" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">L per unit length</text><text x="80" y="90" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">C per unit length</text><text x="200" y="62" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12">Z₀ = √(L/C)</text></svg>

questions.solution: For a lossless transmission line, the telegrapher's equations yield the characteristic impedance Z₀ = √(L/C) with dimensions of ohms. It represents the ratio of voltage to current for a single traveling wave, and is the impedance that results in no reflections.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: On a Smith chart, a constant VSWR circle is centered at the origin. Rotating 180° from the load impedance point along this circle corresponds to the input impedance at what electrical distance from the load?

figure.markup: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 300 280" font-family="serif" font-size="10"><text x="150" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13">Smith Chart (simplified)</text><circle cx="150" cy="140" r="110" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="150" cy="140" r="70" fill="none" stroke="#999" stroke-width="0.7" stroke-dasharray="3"/><circle cx="150" cy="140" r="35" fill="none" stroke="#999" stroke-width="0.7" stroke-dasharray="3"/><line x1="40" y1="140" x2="260" y2="140" stroke="#333" stroke-width="0.8"/><ellipse cx="190" cy="140" rx="35" ry="55" fill="none" stroke="#369" stroke-width="0.8"/><circle cx="195" cy="125" r="4" fill="#c00"/><text x="205" y="123" fill="#c00" font-size="9">Z_L</text><path d="M 195 125 A 42 42 0 1 0 105 155" fill="none" stroke="#090" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4"/><circle cx="105" cy="155" r="4" fill="#090"/><text x="75" y="170" fill="#090" font-size="9">λ/4</text><text x="150" y="270" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">180° rotation = λ/4 distance along line</text></svg>

questions.solution: A full 360° rotation on the Smith chart corresponds to λ/2 along the transmission line. Therefore 180° rotation corresponds to λ/4. At λ/4 from the load, the impedance transforms as Z_in = Z₀²/Z_L, which is the impedance inversion property of a quarter-wave transformer.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The quality factor Q of a resonant cavity increases with:
options: Higher loss tangent of the filling material
Lower operating frequency for a given mode
questions.solution: Q = ω₀ × (energy stored)/(power dissipated). Higher wall conductivity reduces ohmic losses (skin-effect resistance), increasing Q. Smaller cavities decrease stored energy faster than losses. Higher loss tangent increases dielectric losses. For a given mode, lower frequency means larger cavity and different Q scaling.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A plane electromagnetic wave of intensity I is normally incident on a perfectly absorbing surface. The radiation pressure exerted on the surface is:
questions.solution: For a perfectly absorbing surface, the momentum transferred per unit time per unit area is I/c (all incident momentum is absorbed). For a perfectly reflecting surface, it would be 2I/c (momentum reversed). The radiation pressure on a perfect absorber is P = I/c.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A finite symmetric square well of depth V₀ and half-width a always supports at least one bound state. The number of bound states depends on η = √(2mV₀)a/ℏ. For η < π/2, the well supports:

figure.markup: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 300 160" font-family="serif" font-size="11"><text x="150" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13">Finite Square Well</text><line x1="40" y1="40" x2="90" y2="40" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="90" y1="40" x2="90" y2="120" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="90" y1="120" x2="210" y2="120" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="210" y1="120" x2="210" y2="40" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="210" y1="40" x2="260" y2="40" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="150" y="140" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">V = −V₀ for −a < x < +a</text><line x1="90" y1="100" x2="210" y2="100" stroke="#c00" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3"/><text x="150" y="96" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9" fill="#c00">ground state</text></svg>

questions.solution: For a symmetric finite square well, the even-parity bound states satisfy a transcendental equation. One new state appears each time η crosses nπ/2. For η < π/2, only one even-parity bound state exists, and no odd-parity states exist. Thus exactly one bound state is always present regardless of well depth.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A particle of mass m in a one-dimensional attractive delta function potential V(x) = −αδ(x) has exactly one bound state. Its energy is:
questions.solution: Solving −ℏ²ψ''/(2m) − αδ(x)ψ = Eψ with E < 0 gives the bound state energy E = −mα²/(2ℏ²). The wavefunction is ψ(x) = √(κ) e^{−κ|x|} with κ = mα/ℏ². This is the only bound state.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A semiconductor quantum dot is often called an 'artificial atom' because:
options: It contains exactly the same number of electrons as an atom
It has discrete, quantized energy levels analogous to an atomic spectrum
It is always spherical in shape
It is composed of the same elements as natural atoms
questions.solution: Quantum dots confine charge carriers in all three spatial dimensions, producing discrete energy levels similar to atomic orbitals. The energy spacing can be tuned by changing the dot size, but the key analogy to atoms is the discrete (quantized) energy spectrum, not the shape or composition.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In Feynman's path integral formulation, the propagator K(b, a) from point a to point b is obtained by:
options: Summing over all possible paths with phase exp(iS[x]/ℏ)
Solving the time-dependent Schrödinger equation directly
Applying the time-evolution operator to the initial state
questions.solution: Feynman's path integral expresses the propagator as K(b,a) = ∫𝒟[x(t)] exp(iS[x]/ℏ), a sum over all paths from a to b weighted by exp(iS/ℏ). Classical paths dominate in the ℏ → 0 limit via stationary phase, recovering classical mechanics.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The spontaneous magnetization M of a ferromagnet below the Curie temperature T_C in mean field theory varies near T_C as:
questions.solution: In mean field (Brillouin/Weiss) theory, the self-consistent equation for M near T_C yields M ∝ (1 − T/T_C)^{β} with the mean field critical exponent β = 1/2. So M ∝ (T_C − T)^{1/2} just below T_C. The exact 2D Ising value is β = 1/8.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The exact Onsager solution of the 2D Ising model gives the critical exponent β = 1/8 for the spontaneous magnetization. In contrast, mean field theory predicts:
questions.solution: Mean field theory (valid for d ≥ 4 dimensions above the upper critical dimension) gives β = 1/2, while the exact 2D Ising result is β = 1/8. This dramatic difference shows that mean field theory fails in low dimensions where fluctuations are important.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For a photon gas in thermal equilibrium at temperature T, the relationship between radiation pressure P and energy density u is:
questions.solution: Photons are massless bosons with equation of state P = u/3 (in SI: P = u/3). This follows from the Stefan-Boltzmann law and the thermodynamic relation P = −(∂F/∂V)_T with the Helmholtz free energy for a photon gas F = −(π²/45)(kT)⁴V/(ℏc)³.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Rényi entropy of order α is S_α = (1/(1−α)) ln(Σp_i^α). In the limit α → 1, this reduces to:
questions.solution: Taking the limit α → 1 using L'Hôpital's rule: lim_{α→1} (1/(1−α)) ln(Σp_i^α) = −Σp_i ln p_i, which is the Shannon entropy. The Rényi entropy is a one-parameter generalization that includes Shannon entropy as a special case.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Atomic & Molecular Physics · Helium Atom · Variational Method
questions.stem: In the variational treatment of the helium atom ground state using the trial wavefunction ψ = (Z_eff³/π) exp[−Z_eff(r₁ + r₂)] (in atomic units), minimizing the energy expectation value gives the optimal effective nuclear charge Z_eff =:
questions.solution: The energy functional is E(Z_eff) = Z_eff² − 4Z_eff + (5/8)Z_eff = Z_eff² − (27/8)Z_eff. Minimizing: dE/dZ_eff = 2Z_eff − 27/8 = 0, giving Z_eff = 27/16 ≈ 1.6875. This screens the nuclear charge (Z = 2) by the electron cloud, giving the best single-parameter variational energy of −2.8476 hartree (exact: −2.9037).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For electric dipole transitions in a hydrogen-like atom, the selection rules are Δl = ±1, Δm_l = 0, ±1. Which of the following transitions is FORBIDDEN?
questions.solution: The 2s → 1s transition has Δl = 0 (both are s states with l = 0), violating the Δl = ±1 selection rule. This is a forbidden electric dipole transition. The 2s state decays very slowly via two-photon emission (lifetime ~0.12 s).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A nonlinear molecule with N atoms has 3N − 6 normal modes of vibration. For water (H₂O, N = 3), the three vibrational modes are:
options: Symmetric stretch, asymmetric stretch, and bending
One stretch, one bend, one torsion
questions.solution: Water has 3(3) − 6 = 3 vibrational modes: the symmetric O–H stretch (~3657 cm⁻¹), asymmetric O–H stretch (~3756 cm⁻¹), and H–O–H bending mode (~1595 cm⁻¹). All three are infrared active due to the change in dipole moment.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Doppler broadening of a spectral line at temperature T for atoms of mass m and rest wavelength λ₀ has a full width at half maximum Δλ_D proportional to:
questions.solution: The Doppler width is Δν_D = (ν₀/c)√(2kT ln 2/m), or equivalently Δλ_D = (λ₀/c)√(2kT ln 2/m) ∝ √(T/m). Higher temperature or lighter mass increases the thermal velocity spread, broadening the line.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In Doppler-free saturation spectroscopy, a strong pump beam and a weak counter-propagating probe beam produce a 'Lamb dip' at the center of the absorption line. The Lamb dip occurs because:
options: Both beams interact with the same zero-velocity class of atoms
The pump beam creates a population inversion
The probe beam is amplified by stimulated emission
questions.solution: The pump saturates the transition for atoms with v = 0 (the velocity class that sees both beams at the same frequency). When the probe scans through the same transition, it finds reduced absorption for v = 0 atoms (already depleted by the pump), creating a narrow dip within the broader Doppler profile.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Hall coefficient R_H for a metal with free electron density n is R_H = −1/(ne). For a p-type semiconductor where holes are the majority carriers, R_H is:
questions.solution: For holes (charge +e) as majority carriers, R_H = +1/(pe) > 0. The sign of R_H directly indicates the type of charge carrier: negative for electrons, positive for holes. This is the standard method for determining carrier type in semiconductors.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In a semiconductor with both electrons and holes as carriers, the transverse magnetoresistance:
options: Does not vanish even for a spherical Fermi surface
Is always zero due to compensation
Depends only on the total carrier concentration
Is independent of the magnetic field
questions.solution: For a single carrier type with a spherical Fermi surface, the magnetoresistance vanishes exactly. However, with two carrier types (electrons and holes), the different Lorentz deflections prevent complete cancellation, and the magnetoresistance is nonzero and generally increases with B.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Peierls instability in a one-dimensional metal at low temperature leads to a lattice distortion that:
options: Opens a band gap at the Fermi level, making the system insulating
questions.solution: A 1D metal with a half-filled band is unstable to a periodic lattice distortion (dimerization) that opens a gap at the Fermi level. The energy gained by opening the gap exceeds the elastic energy cost, resulting in a metal-to-insulator (Peierls) transition at temperature T_P.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the two-fluid model of superfluid helium (He-II), the superfluid component:
options: Has zero viscosity and carries no entropy
Has high viscosity and carries entropy
Has zero viscosity and carries entropy
Has high viscosity and carries no entropy
questions.solution: The two-fluid model describes He-II as a mixture of a normal component (with viscosity and entropy) and a superfluid component (with exactly zero viscosity and zero entropy). The superfluid component flows without dissipation and cannot carry thermal energy.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a differential amplifier is defined as:

figure.markup: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 320 130" font-family="serif" font-size="11"><text x="160" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13">Differential Amplifier</text><rect x="70" y="35" width="180" height="60" rx="4" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="160" y="70" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">Differential Amplifier</text><line x1="15" y1="55" x2="70" y2="55" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="15" y1="80" x2="70" y2="80" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="250" y1="65" x2="305" y2="65" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="8" y="52" font-size="9">V⁺</text><text x="8" y="85" font-size="9">V⁻</text><text x="310" y="70" font-size="9">V_out</text><text x="160" y="120" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">CMRR = A_d / A_cm (typically in dB)</text></svg>

questions.solution: CMRR = A_d/A_cm, where A_d is the differential-mode gain and A_cm is the common-mode gain. In dB: CMRR_dB = 20 log₁₀(A_d/A_cm). A high CMRR means the amplifier effectively rejects signals common to both inputs while amplifying the difference.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A phase-locked loop (PLL) consists of a phase detector, a loop filter, and a voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO). When the PLL achieves lock, the VCO output frequency:

figure.markup: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 360 130" font-family="serif" font-size="10"><text x="180" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13">Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)</text><rect x="10" y="40" width="75" height="35" rx="3" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.2"/><text x="47" y="57" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9">Phase</text><text x="47" y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9">Detector</text><rect x="120" y="40" width="75" height="35" rx="3" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.2"/><text x="157" y="57" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9">Loop</text><text x="157" y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-size="9">Filter</text><rect x="230" y="40" width="75" height="35" rx="3" fill="none" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.2"/><text x="267" y="62" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">VCO</text><line x1="85" y1="57" x2="120" y2="57" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.2"/><line x1="195" y1="57" x2="230" y2="57" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.2"/><line x1="267" y1="75" x2="267" y2="105" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.2"/><line x1="267" y1="105" x2="47" y2="105" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.2"/><line x1="47" y1="105" x2="47" y2="75" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.2"/><text x="260" y="38" font-size="9">output</text><text x="180" y="125" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">VCO freq locks to input reference freq</text></svg>

options: Is independent of the input frequency
questions.solution: In lock, the phase detector output (error signal) averages to zero, meaning the VCO frequency equals the input reference frequency. The PLL can also use frequency dividers in the feedback path to generate output frequencies that are integer multiples of the reference.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A sigma-delta (ΣΔ) analog-to-digital converter achieves high resolution by employing:
options: Oversampling combined with noise shaping and digital decimation filtering
Successive approximation with a binary search algorithm
Parallel flash comparators for single-cycle conversion
A sample-and-hold circuit with pipeline stages
questions.solution: ΣΔ ADCs oversample the input (typically at 64× to 256× the Nyquist rate) and use a modulator to shape quantization noise toward high frequencies. A digital decimation filter then removes the out-of-band noise, achieving effective resolution far exceeding the quantizer's bit depth.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) serial communication protocol uses how many signal lines?
questions.solution: I²C uses exactly two wires: SDA (Serial Data) and SCL (Serial Clock). Both are open-drain with pull-up resistors. The protocol supports multi-master and multi-slave configurations with 7-bit or 10-bit addressing, all on just two lines.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In a series linear voltage regulator, the pass transistor maintains a constant output voltage by:
options: Operating in its active region and dissipating excess voltage as heat
Switching rapidly between saturation and cutoff
Using a transformer to step down the voltage
Clipping the voltage with a zener diode only
questions.solution: A series linear regulator uses a pass transistor in its active (linear) region as a variable resistor. An error amplifier compares the output to a reference and adjusts the transistor's base drive to maintain constant output. The excess voltage (V_in − V_out) × I_load is dissipated as heat.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The nuclear radius R is related to the mass number A by R = R₀A^{1/3} with R₀ ≈ 1.2 fm. This A^{1/3} dependence implies that nuclear matter:
options: Has approximately constant density for all nuclei
Becomes less dense for heavier nuclei
Becomes more dense for heavier nuclei
questions.solution: Since V ∝ R³ ∝ A and mass M ∝ A, the density ρ = M/V is approximately constant for all nuclei. This demonstrates that nuclear matter is nearly incompressible — adding nucleons increases the volume proportionally, keeping the density ~2.3 × 10¹⁷ kg/m³.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Geiger-Nuttall law relates the decay constant λ of alpha emitters to the alpha particle energy E_α. The empirical relation is:
questions.solution: The Geiger-Nuttall law states log₁₀λ = a + b/√E_α, where a and b are constants for a given isotopic series. Higher alpha energy leads to much shorter half-lives because the tunneling probability through the Coulomb barrier increases exponentially with energy.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The continuous energy spectrum of electrons emitted in beta decay led Pauli to postulate the neutrino. The maximum kinetic energy in the beta spectrum corresponds to:
options: The case where the antineutrino carries zero kinetic energy
The case where the antineutrino carries maximum energy
The average energy of the antineutrino
The rest mass energy of the antineutrino
questions.solution: By energy-momentum conservation, the electron and antineutrino share the available energy. The electron has maximum kinetic energy when the antineutrino carries zero kinetic energy (is emitted with near-zero momentum). This endpoint energy equals Q − m_e c².

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Homestake experiment (Davis et al.) detected solar neutrinos via ³⁷Cl + ν_e → ³⁷Ar + e⁻. The observed neutrino flux was approximately:
options: One-third of the theoretical prediction (solar neutrino problem)
questions.solution: Homestake detected about 1/3 of the predicted ⁸B solar neutrinos. This 'solar neutrino problem' was resolved by the SNO experiment, which showed that electron neutrinos oscillate into muon and tau neutrinos during transit from the Sun.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Quark confinement implies that the potential between two quarks at large separation r behaves approximately as:
options: V(r) = −4α_s/(3r) + κr, where κ is the string tension
questions.solution: The quark-antiquark potential has a Coulomb-like term −4α_s/(3r) at short distances (one-gluon exchange) and a linearly rising term κr at large distances (confinement). The string tension κ ≈ 0.18 GeV² means infinite energy is needed to separate quarks, explaining why free quarks are never observed.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In quantum electrodynamics (QED), the vertex factor for the interaction between an electron and a photon is proportional to:

figure.markup: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 280 140" font-family="serif" font-size="11"><text x="140" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13">QED Electron-Photon Vertex</text><line x1="30" y1="95" x2="110" y2="50" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="110" y1="50" x2="190" y2="95" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M 110 50 Q 130 15 150 50" fill="none" stroke="#c00" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="110" cy="50" r="3.5" fill="#333"/><text x="20" y="110" font-size="10">e⁻ (in)</text><text x="175" y="110" font-size="10">e⁻ (out)</text><text x="155" y="25" font-size="10" fill="#c00">γ</text><text x="140" y="132" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10">Vertex: ieγ^μ</text></svg>

options: ieγ^μ (where e is the coupling, γ^μ Dirac matrices)
questions.solution: The QED vertex factor is ieγ^μ, where e is the electromagnetic coupling constant and γ^μ are the Dirac gamma matrices. This vertex, combined with propagators and external leg factors, forms the building blocks for computing QED scattering amplitudes to any order in perturbation theory.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A student scored 72% in an exam where the passing mark is 60%. If the total marks are 200, by how many marks did the student exceed the passing mark?
questions.solution: Student scored: 72% of 200 = 144. Passing: 60% of 200 = 120. Excess = 144 − 120 = 24 marks.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A can complete a task in 12 days and B can complete it in 18 days. Working together, how many days will they take to finish the work?
questions.solution: A's rate = 1/12 per day; B's rate = 1/18 per day. Combined = 1/12 + 1/18 = 3/36 + 2/36 = 5/36 per day. Days = 36/5 = 7.2 days.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The average of five numbers is 42. If one number is excluded, the average becomes 38. What is the excluded number?
questions.solution: Total of 5 numbers = 5 × 42 = 210. Total of 4 numbers = 4 × 38 = 152. Excluded number = 210 − 152 = 58.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If A:B = 3:5 and B:C = 4:7, what is A:B:C?
questions.solution: A:B = 3:5 and B:C = 4:7. Make B equal: A:B = 12:20 and B:C = 20:35. So A:B:C = 12:20:35.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A car travels from city A to city B at 60 km/h and returns at 40 km/h. What is the average speed for the entire journey?
questions.solution: For equal distances: average speed = 2v₁v₂/(v₁ + v₂) = 2(60)(40)/(60 + 40) = 4800/100 = 48 km/h.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Two fair dice are thrown simultaneously. What is the probability that the sum of the numbers is 7?
questions.solution: Favorable outcomes for sum 7: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1) = 6 outcomes. Total outcomes = 36. Probability = 6/36 = 1/6.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In how many ways can 5 books be arranged on a shelf?
questions.solution: Number of arrangements = 5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What is the remainder when 2²⁵ is divided by 7?
questions.solution: 2¹ mod 7 = 2, 2² mod 7 = 4, 2³ mod 7 = 1. Cycle of 3. 25 = 3×8 + 1, so 2²⁵ mod 7 = 2¹ mod 7 = 2.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If x + 1/x = 3, what is the value of x² + 1/x²?
questions.solution: Squaring: (x + 1/x)² = x² + 2 + 1/x² = 9. So x² + 1/x² = 9 − 2 = 7.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The area of a triangle with vertices at (0,0), (6,0), and (3,4) is:
questions.solution: Area = ½|x₁(y₂−y₃) + x₂(y₃−y₁) + x₃(y₁−y₂)| = ½|0(0−4) + 6(4−0) + 3(0−0)| = ½|24| = 12 sq units.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In what ratio should rice costing Rs.40/kg be mixed with rice costing Rs.60/kg to get a mixture costing Rs.52/kg?
questions.solution: By alligation: (60−52):(52−40) = 8:12 = 2:3. So the ratio is 2:3.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A boat can travel 20 km upstream in 5 hours and 20 km downstream in 2 hours. What is the speed of the current?
questions.solution: Upstream speed = 20/5 = 4 km/h. Downstream speed = 20/2 = 10 km/h. Current = (downstream − upstream)/2 = (10 − 4)/2 = 3 km/h.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Pipe A can fill a tank in 10 hours and Pipe B can empty it in 15 hours. If both are opened simultaneously, how long will it take to fill the tank?
questions.solution: A's fill rate = 1/10 per hour. B's empty rate = 1/15 per hour. Net rate = 1/10 − 1/15 = 3/30 − 2/30 = 1/30 per hour. Time = 30 hours.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In a code language, if APPLE = 50 and ORANGE = 60, then HAVEN = ?
questions.solution: The code assigns each letter its position in the alphabet (A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26) and sums them. APPLE: 1+16+16+12+5 = 50. ORANGE: 15+18+1+14+7+5 = 60. HAVEN: 8+1+22+5+14 = 50.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Five persons A, B, C, D, E are sitting in a row facing north. C sits exactly in the middle. A sits to the immediate left of C. D sits to the immediate right of E. Who sits at the leftmost position?
questions.solution: Positions 1–5 (left to right). C at position 3. A immediately left of C → A at position 2. D immediately right of E → D = E + 1. Available positions: 1, 4, 5. Only valid assignment: E at 4, D at 5. B at position 1 (leftmost).

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Statements: All dogs are animals. All animals are living beings. Conclusion I: All dogs are living beings. Conclusion II: Some living beings are dogs. Which conclusion(s) follow?
questions.solution: From the chain All dogs → All animals → All living beings, it follows that All dogs are living beings (I). Since all dogs are living beings, certainly some living beings are dogs (II). Both conclusions follow logically.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Pointing to a photograph, a man says: 'She is the daughter of the only son of my father.' How is the person in the photograph related to the man?
questions.solution: The only son of the man's father is the man himself. So 'the daughter of the only son of my father' is the man's own daughter.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Rahul starts from point A, walks 5 km north, then turns right and walks 3 km, then turns right and walks 5 km. In which direction is he from the starting point A?
questions.solution: From A: 5 km north → 3 km east (right) → 5 km south (right). Net displacement: 3 km east of A. Direction: East.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What is the next number in the series: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ?
questions.solution: Differences: 4, 6, 8, 10, 12. The next difference is 12, so next term = 30 + 12 = 42. Alternatively, n(n+1): 1×2=2, 2×3=6, 3×4=12, 4×5=20, 5×6=30, 6×7=42.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Doctor : Hospital :: Teacher : ?
questions.solution: A doctor works in a hospital. A teacher works in a school. The relationship is professional : workplace.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Which does NOT belong to the group? (Apple, Mango, Potato, Banana)
questions.solution: Apple, Mango, and Banana are fruits. Potato is a vegetable (tuber). Potato is the odd one out.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If 1st January 2025 is a Wednesday, what day of the week is 1st March 2025?
questions.solution: Jan has 31 days, Feb has 28 days (2025 is not a leap year). Days from Jan 1 to Mar 1 = 31 + 28 = 59 days. 59 mod 7 = 3. Wednesday + 3 = Saturday.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the sentence with no grammatical error:
options: Neither the teacher nor the students was present.
Each of the boys have completed their assignment.
The committee has decided to approve the proposal.
She don't know the answer to the question.
questions.solution: 'The committee has decided' is correct — 'committee' is a collective noun taking a singular verb. Other options: (A) should use 'were', (B) should use 'has', (D) should use 'doesn't'.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: What does the idiom 'to burn the midnight oil' mean?
options: To work late into the night
questions.solution: 'Burn the midnight oil' means to work or study late into the night, originally referring to working by oil lamp light before electricity.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: A person who loves books is called a:
questions.solution: Bibliophile = lover of books. Philatelist = stamp collector. Numismatist = coin collector. Cinephile = movie enthusiast.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct preposition: 'The meeting is _____ Monday.'
questions.solution: We use 'on' with days of the week: on Monday, on Tuesday, etc.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Read the passage: 'The monsoon is a seasonal wind pattern that brings heavy rainfall to the Indian subcontinent. It is driven by the differential heating of land and sea.' What primarily drives the monsoon?
options: Differential heating of land and sea
questions.solution: The passage explicitly states the monsoon is driven by the differential heating of land and sea, which creates pressure gradients that reverse wind direction seasonally.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'By the time we arrived, the train _____.'
questions.solution: Past perfect 'had left' is used for an action completed before another past action ('arrived'). The sequence: train left → we arrived.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most similar in meaning to 'ELOQUENT':
questions.solution: Eloquent means fluent, persuasive, and expressive in speaking or writing. It implies the ability to communicate effectively and beautifully.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: She has been working here _____ 2019.
questions.solution: 'Since' is used with a specific point in time (2019). 'For' is used with a duration (5 years). 'From' needs 'to/until' for a range.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most opposite in meaning to 'BENEVOLENT':
questions.solution: Benevolent means well-meaning and kindly. Malevolent means having or showing a wish to do evil to others — the direct opposite.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct passive voice: 'The teacher is correcting the papers.'
options: The papers are being corrected by the teacher.
The papers were being corrected by the teacher.
The papers have been corrected by the teacher.
The papers is being corrected by the teacher.
questions.solution: Present continuous active 'is correcting' → present continuous passive 'are being corrected'. The subject 'papers' is plural, so 'are' is used.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Which Indian space agency successfully landed Chandrayaan-3 on the Moon's south pole in August 2023?
questions.solution: ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation) successfully soft-landed Chandrayaan-3's Vikram lander near the Moon's south pole on August 23, 2023, making India the fourth country to achieve a soft lunar landing.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) maintains a policy repo rate. As of early 2024, the repo rate stood at:
questions.solution: The RBI maintained the repo rate at 6.50% through its February 2024 policy review, having paused rate hikes since February 2023 after a series of increases from 4.00% in early 2022.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's first indigenous aircraft carrier, commissioned in 2022, is named:
questions.solution: INS Vikrant (R11) was commissioned on September 2, 2022. It is India's first indigenous aircraft carrier and the first warship to be designed and built in India, displacing approximately 45,000 tonnes.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Which of the following is the longest river in peninsular India?
questions.solution: The Godavari River, at approximately 1,465 km, is the longest river in peninsular India. It rises in Maharashtra and flows through Telangana and Andhra Pradesh before draining into the Bay of Bengal.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act deals with:
questions.solution: The 73rd Amendment (1992) constitutionalized Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRIs), establishing a three-tier local self-government system in rural India with provisions for reservations and regular elections.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The GST (Goods and Services Tax) in India was implemented from:
questions.solution: India's GST was implemented on July 1, 2017, replacing multiple indirect taxes with a unified tax structure. The Constitutional 101st Amendment Act enabled this reform.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The G20 summit held in India in September 2023 took place in which city?
questions.solution: The 18th G20 Leaders' Summit was held on September 9–10, 2023, at the Bharat Mandapam in New Delhi. Under India's presidency, the African Union was admitted as a permanent member of the G20.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Which Indian mission was launched to study the Sun's corona and solar wind?
questions.solution: Aditya-L1 was launched by ISRO on September 2, 2023, and placed at the Sun-Earth Lagrange point L1 on January 6, 2024. It carries seven scientific payloads to study the Sun's corona, chromosphere, and solar wind.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Which Indian state has the longest coastline?
questions.solution: Gujarat has the longest coastline among Indian states at approximately 1,600 km, including the Gulf of Kutch and Gulf of Khambhat. Andhra Pradesh is second with about 974 km.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The UPI (Unified Payments Interface) system was developed by:
questions.solution: UPI was developed by the National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI), which operates under the guidance of the RBI. Launched in April 2016, UPI has revolutionized digital payments in India.
